The patient is positioned in a steep head-low (Trendelenburg) position, allowing the bowel to shift towards the diaphragm. The surgical field is inspected, revealing adhesions in the appendicular area and colitic adhesions. The liver appears normal. Port placement is confirmed with two ports on the surgeon's side and one on the assistant's side, with the lower port positioned one inch above and medial to the anterior superior iliac spine.

The surgeon visually inspects the uterine tubes to identify the location of fibroids. The left tube is visible, with a large fibroid on the posterior wall. The right tube is also visible, with a large fibroid on the anterior wall.

The surgeon identifies a corneal fibroid on the uterus, with the fallopian tube visible nearby. There are three small fibroids and two large fibroids present. A vasopressor injection needle is introduced, and the surgeon injects into the anterior myometrium, penetrating half an inch, followed by aspiration and injection of the solution.

The surgeon injects 30 ml of vasoconstrictor into the uterus, ensuring it is not entering a venous sinus or vessel. Approximately 50 ml has been injected, with plans to use around 300 to 350 ml based on the uterus's appearance. The pulse remains stable throughout the procedure.

The surgeon observes the uterus, noting that 300 ml of vasopressin solution has been injected, causing the upper fibroid to appear white. An elliptical incision is made to remove the posterior fibroid, which shows signs of myometrial hypertrophy and degeneration. Despite some bleeding, the surgeon prepares to suture the posterior wall after removing the fibroid.

The surgeon identifies a larger fibroid on the anterior side, visible due to saline surrounding it. The fibroid is not trimmed, as the broad ligament tissue available for suturing decreases once the fibroid is delivered.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw to apply traction to a larger fibroid, making the surgical plane easier to access. A tooth needle holder is used to provide counter traction, ensuring stability during the procedure.

The surgeon advances the myoma screw on the fibroid, which is then removed, revealing a normal-appearing uterus. The myoma screw is slightly bent due to the force applied, and the surgeon adjusts it. There is no bleeding anteriorly, but some posterior bleeding prompts the surgeon to consider cauterization. A small incision is made on the posterior wall to remove the fibroid, avoiding a tunneling incision to minimize myometrial trauma.

The surgeon observes hypertrophy of the anterior fibroid and notes the presence of another fibroid. Approximately fifty milliliters of blood loss is noted from the posterior fibroid, with no significant bleeding observed. A double needle suture, 24 by 4 centimeters, using a barbed suture (Stratafix by Johnson & Johnson) is prepared for use.

The surgeon begins suturing the posterior uterine wall, using a needle to create a full-thickness bite without going unnecessarily deep. The suture is held to retract the tissue, aided by the weight of the needle holder. The procedure involves a double-layer closure, ensuring adequate repair of the uterine wall. The surgeon plans to suture the anterior wall next and search for a smaller fibroid located there.

The surgeon performs a closure of the upper part of the incision using an inside-out stitch technique. The needle is turned and passed gently through the tissue to achieve the desired suture line. A deeper stitch is applied, and the surgeon completes the closure by returning to the original incision line.

The surgeon adjusts the tension on the suture thread to achieve optimal closure of the posterior uterine wall, ensuring it is not overly tightened. Two additional sutures are placed to complete the closure, followed by cutting the thread with scissors.

The surgeon pulls the second needle in and ties it to finish the anterior suturing, requiring a triple layer due to the increased depth compared to the posterior part. The anterior section shows no bleeding, likely due to the vasopressin effect. The incision, initially appearing oblique, has shifted to a vertical orientation after fibroid removal, making closure more challenging.

The surgical field shows improved exposure of the tissue, with the first layer of closure completed. The surgeon notes the difficulty in suturing due to the tough tissue, similar to challenges faced in adenomyosis procedures.

The surgeon performs a baseball stitch on a superficial layer of the uterine tissue, using the right hand to take a bite from the right side. The surgeon then prepares to take a bite from the left side, demonstrating the technique of inside-out suturing.

The surgeon takes two more bites to complete the suturing, managing a short thread to tie it up. Both anterior and posterior sutures are completed, and the surgeon considers searching for another anterior fibroid.

Three fibroids have been removed, and the surgeon uses a myoma screw and Grasperani wash to inspect the surgical site. Blood is visible in a peritoneal fold, but the anterior area appears satisfactory. The surgeon evaluates a small fibroid to determine if it can be easily extracted.

The surgeon injects the remaining solution into the uterus, causing it to turn white, and observes leakage from the cut open myometrium. A transverse incision is made to access a small seedling fibroid, which is similar in size to a previously removed posterior fibroid. The surgeon plans to close the incision using a figure-of-eight suture with 2-0 vicryl, sufficient for the anterior wall closure.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to touch the tissue without suturing deeply, opting for two figure-of-eight sutures for closure. A cutting needle is used for better control and ease of passage through the tissue, ensuring precise directionality. The surgeon assesses the tightness of the sutures, noting that a barbed suture is not used, and determines that one more bite is required for completion.

The surgeon tightens the barb suture line step by step, ensuring it remains tight without cutting through the tissue. The suture line branches and turns white as it is tightened. Surface coagulation is performed on the seedling fibroid using bipolar, avoiding damage to the deeper myometrium. A quick wash follows before proceeding with morcellation.

The surgeon observes mild oozing from the surgical site, noting that it is not significant enough to warrant placing a drain or additional sutures. The anterior aspect appears fine after removing blood from the pouch. The surgical field is reassessed for blood accumulation post-morcellation.

The surgeon uses a morcellator to extract a long strip of tissue with a consistent texture, ensuring the prongs are properly closed to avoid damaging the blades. The tissue is neither too soft nor too hard, and the surgeon pulls on the grasper to facilitate cutting.

The surgeon completes the morcellation and removes the morcellator from the surgical field. The thumb is placed on the port to prevent leakage, and a 5mm port is reinserted. The surgeon examines the pouch, revealing a collection of fluid rather than blood, and notes mild oozing. The concern is potential shoulder pain due to peritoneal irritation from any blood present in the cavity.

The surgical field is clear with no visible blood accumulation on either side. Any small fluid collection from previous oozing has been removed from the pouch. The surgeon performs a head-high maneuver and wash to clear smaller tissue chips towards the pelvis. The appendix is visually confirmed to be normal, with no signs of appendicitis or adhesion.

The surgeon observes a minimal ooze from the needle puncture site at the end of suturing, noting that touching it once with the instrument generally stops the bleeding entirely. The decision is made not to place a drain due to the mild nature of the ooze.

The surgeon removes the cannula from a 5mm puncture site and inserts a Ryle tube to potentially place a drain. A syringe is used to inject 10cc of ropivacaine into the port site and another 10cc into the peritoneal cavity. The needle is visible as it is inserted just above the peritoneum, below the muscle layer.

The surgeon prepares to close the morcellator port by placing a needle to avoid the muscle, taking a bite of tissue. The needle is positioned with insufflation on, ensuring proper placement before tightening after the abdomen collapses.
The surgeon uses 2-0 vicryl sutures to knot the muscle carefully, ensuring not to strangulate the tissue. Skin staplers are applied for the closure of the skin.
